content:
# Genesis Chapter 2 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 2r-2v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_002_page_001.jpg, GEN_002_page_002.jpg

---

**2:1** And-were-finished the-heavens and-the-earth and-all their-host.

**2:2** And-finished God in-the-day the-seventh His-work which He-had-made, and-He-rested in-the-day the-seventh from-all His-work which He-had-made.

**2:3** And-blessed God the-day the-seventh, and-sanctified it, for in-it He-rested from-all His-work which created God to-make.

**2:4** These [are] the-generations of-the-heavens and-of-the-earth in-their-being-created, in-[the]-day of-making YHWH God earth and-heavens.

**2:5** And-every shrub of-the-field was-not-yet in-the-earth, and-every herb of-the-field had-not-yet sprouted, for not had-caused-to-rain YHWH God upon the-earth, and-man there-was-none to-work the-ground.

**2:6** And-a-mist was-going-up from the-earth, and-it-watered all [the]-face of-the-ground.

**2:7** And-formed YHWH God the-man [of]-dust from the-ground, and-He-breathed into-his-nostrils [the]-breath of-life, and-became the-man a-soul living.

**2:8** And-planted YHWH God a-garden in-Eden from-east, and-He-placed there the-man whom He-had-formed.

**2:9** And-caused-to-sprout YHWH God from the-ground every tree pleasant to-sight and-good for-food, and-[the]-tree of-the-life in-[the]-midst of-the-garden, and-[the]-tree of-the-knowledge of-good and-evil.

**2:10** And-a-river [was] going-out from-Eden to-water the-garden, and-from-there it-was-divided and-became to-four heads.

**2:11** [The]-name of-the-one [is] Pishon; it [is] the-one-surrounding all [the]-land of-the-Havilah, where there [is] the-gold.

**2:12** And-[the]-gold of-the-land the-that [is] good; there [is] the-bdellium and-[the]-stone of-the-onyx.

**2:13** And-[the]-name of-the-river the-second [is] Gihon; it [is] the-one-surrounding all [the]-land of-Cush.

**2:14** And-[the]-name of-the-river the-third [is] Hiddeqel [Tigris]; it [is] the-one-going east-of Asshur. And-the-river the-fourth, it [is] Phrath [Euphrates].

**2:15** And-took YHWH God the-man, and-placed-him in-[the]-garden of-Eden, to-work-it and-to-keep-it.

**2:16** And-commanded YHWH God upon the-man, saying, "From-every tree of-the-garden eating you-shall-eat.

**2:17** But-from-[the]-tree of-the-knowledge of-good and-evil, you-shall-not eat from-it, for in-[the]-day of-your-eating from-it, dying you-shall-die."

**2:18** And-said YHWH God, "Not good [is]-the-being of-the-man alone-of-him; I-will-make for-him a-helper as-opposite-him."

**2:19** And-formed YHWH God from the-ground every beast of-the-field and-every bird of-the-heavens, and-He-brought [them] to the-man to-see what he-would-call to-it, and-all which would-call to-it the-man [as]-a-living soul, that [was] its-name.

**2:20** And-called the-man names to-all the-cattle and-to-[the]-bird of-the-heavens and-to-every beast of-the-field; but-for-Adam, not he-found a-helper as-opposite-him.

**2:21** And-caused-to-fall YHWH God a-deep-sleep upon the-man, and-he-slept, and-He-took one from-his-ribs, and-closed-up flesh in-its-place.

**2:22** And-built YHWH God the-rib which He-had-taken from the-man into-a-woman, and-He-brought-her to the-man.

**2:23** And-said the-man, "This [one], this-time, [is] bone from-my-bones and-flesh from-my-flesh; to-this-one it-shall-be-called Woman [Ishshah], for from-Man [Ish] was-taken this-one."

**2:24** Upon-thus shall-leave a-man his-father and-his-mother, and-shall-cling to-his-woman, and-they-shall-become to-flesh one.

**2:25** And-they-were, [the]-two-of-them, naked, the-man and-his-woman, and-not they-were-ashamed.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **2:2 -- "in-the-day the-seventh"**: The Leningrad Codex reads *ba-yom ha-shevi'i* (בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י) -- "in the seventh day" -- for when God finished His work. The Samaritan Pentateuch, the Septuagint (LXX), and the Syriac Peshitta all read "the sixth day" (*ha-shishi*) here, apparently to avoid the implication that God was still working on the seventh day. The Leningrad Codex preserves the harder reading, which is to be preferred by the principle of *lectio difficilior*. -- EMET

2. **2:4 -- Toledot formula**: The word *toledot* (תוֹלְד֧וֹת) literally means "begettings" or "generations." This is the first occurrence of the formula "these are the generations of..." which structures the entire book of Genesis. The Leningrad Codex spells it here defectively (*toldot*) without the expected *waw* after the *taw*, as is characteristic of this codex. -- EMET

3. **2:5 -- *terem* construction**: The Hebrew *terem* (טֶ֗רֶם) with the imperfect (*yihyeh*, *yitsmach*) describes a state that had "not yet" come to be. This is an archaic Hebrew temporal construction. I have rendered it "was-not-yet" to preserve its force. -- EMET

4. **2:6 -- *'ed* (אֵ֖ד)**:  This word appears only here and in Job 36:27. Its meaning is debated. Traditional rendering is "mist" or "vapor." Akkadian cognate *edu* suggests "flood" or "underground water surge." Sumerian parallel may be *id* ("river"). I have retained "mist" as the most traditional rendering but flag the uncertainty. -- EMET

5. **2:7 -- Double *yod* in *wayyitzer* (וַיִּ֩יצֶר֩)**: The Leningrad Codex writes the verb "and-formed" with two *yods* here, whereas in 2:19 (forming the animals) it is written with only one *yod*. The Masoretic tradition preserved in midrash (Genesis Rabbah 14:11) understands the double *yod* as signifying that man was formed with two inclinations (*yetzer*) -- the good and the evil -- while animals have only one. -- EMET

6. **2:8 -- *mi-qedem* (מִקֶּ֑דֶם)**: This can mean either "from-east" (directional) or "from-of-old" / "from-aforetime" (temporal). Both readings are ancient. I have rendered "from-east" following the geographical context, but the temporal sense ("from-before" / "in-the-beginning") cannot be excluded. -- EMET

7. **2:12 -- *ha-bedolach* (הַבְּדֹ֑לַח)**: Traditionally "bdellium," an aromatic resin. The LXX renders it *anthrax* ("carbuncle"), while some identify it with pearls. The word also appears in Numbers 11:7 describing manna. I have transliterated as "bdellium" following convention. -- EMET

8. **2:12 -- *'even ha-shoham* (אֶ֥בֶן הַשֹּֽׁהַם)**: The identification of *shoham* stone is uncertain. Variously rendered as "onyx," "lapis lazuli," "carnelian," or "beryl." The LXX has *prasinos* ("leek-green stone"). I have rendered "onyx" following the traditional English convention while noting the uncertainty. -- EMET

9. **2:14 -- *Hiddeqel* (חִדֶּ֔קֶל)**: Identified as the Tigris from Akkadian *Idiqlat*. The parenthetical [Tigris] is my gloss. *Phrath* (פְּרָ֖ת) is the Euphrates, from Akkadian *Purattu*. -- EMET

10. **2:17 -- Infinitive absolute construction**: *mot tamut* (מ֥וֹת תָּמֽוּת) -- "dying you-shall-die." The infinitive absolute + finite verb is a Hebrew emphatic construction expressing certainty or intensity. I have preserved the doubling rather than smoothing to "you shall surely die." -- EMET

11. **2:18 -- *kenegdo* (כְּנֶגְדּֽוֹ)**: Literally "as-opposite-him" or "as-his-counterpart." The preposition *neged* means "opposite, in front of, corresponding to." This compound expresses both correspondence (matching) and distinction (facing). I render literally "as-opposite-him" rather than the traditional "suitable for him" or "meet for him," which lose the spatial-relational imagery. -- EMET

12. **2:19 -- *wayyitzer* with single *yod***: Unlike 2:7 where the verb has a double *yod* (וַיִּ֩יצֶר֩), here in 2:19 the Leningrad Codex writes it with a single *yod* (וַיִּצֶר֩). See note 5 above. -- EMET

13. **2:20 -- *le-'Adam* (לְאָדָ֕ם)**: This is the first instance in Genesis where *'adam* may function as a proper name ("Adam") rather than the generic "the-man." The absence of the definite article *ha-* here is significant. I have rendered "for-Adam" to mark this shift. -- EMET

14. **2:21 -- *tardemah* (תַּרְדֵּמָ֛ה)**: A "deep sleep" -- not ordinary sleep. This word appears in contexts of divine activity (cf. Gen 15:12; 1 Sam 26:12; Isa 29:10). It connotes a supernaturally induced unconsciousness. -- EMET

15. **2:22 -- *wayyiben* (וַיִּבֶן֩)**: The verb is *banah* -- "to build," not "to make" or "to form." God *built* the woman from the rib. This is a distinct verb from *yatsar* ("form," used for man in 2:7) and *'asah* ("make"). The architectural metaphor is deliberate and should not be softened. -- EMET

16. **2:23 -- *Ishshah / Ish* wordplay**: The Hebrew *'ishshah* (אִשָּׁ֔ה, "woman") is here etymologically connected to *'ish* (אִ֖ישׁ, "man"). Modern linguists note these words have different roots (*'ishshah* from *'anash*; *'ish* likely from *'ysh*), but the text presents a deliberate folk-etymological wordplay. I preserve it with brackets: "Woman [Ishshah] ... Man [Ish]." -- EMET

17. **2:23 -- *zo't ha-pa'am* (זֹ֣את הַפַּ֗עַם)**: Literally "this the-time" or "this-time." The phrase expresses an exclamation -- "This time!" or "At last!" -- implying that the man had been searching among the animals (2:20) and found none suitable, but now at last recognizes his counterpart. -- EMET

18. **2:25 -- *'arummim* (עֲרוּמִּ֔ים)**: "Naked" -- plural. This word sets up a wordplay with *'arum* (עָר֔וּם, "cunning/shrewd") in 3:1, linking the innocence of the couple with the craftiness of the serpent. The two words share the same consonantal root (*'-r-m*) but with different vowel patterns. -- EMET

19. **2:3 -- *la'asot* (לַעֲשֽׂוֹת)**: The infinitive construct "to-make" at the end of v. 3 is syntactically unusual: "which God created to-make." The phrase *bara' ... la'asot* seems redundant. Some take it as expressing purpose ("created in order to make"), others as a hendiadys ("creatively made"). I have preserved the literal awkwardness. -- EMET

20. **Setumah and Petuchah markers**: The Leningrad Codex marks a *petuchah* (open paragraph, פ) after 2:3, indicating a major division. This corresponds to the transition from the creation week (1:1--2:3) to the Eden narrative (2:4ff.). A *setumah* (closed paragraph, ס) appears after 2:6, marking a minor division before the formation of man. These are visible in the manuscript as spacing conventions. -- EMET

---

## Supplementary Notes — Additional Manuscripts
**Review date:** 2026-03-04 | **Reviewer:** EMET
**Additional sources examined:**
- Dead Sea Scrolls: 4QGen-a (4Q1), 4QGen-b (4Q2)
- Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE
- **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209
- **View DSS online:** https://www.deadseascrolls.org.il/

---

### Prefatory Statement

This addendum supplements the primary EMET translation notes above. It records the testimony of two additional manuscript traditions against the readings of the Leningrad Codex (B19A) already used as the base text: (1) the Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 2 — principally 4QGen-a (4Q1) and 4QGen-b (4Q2), copied c. 100–50 BCE and thus predating the Leningrad Codex by over 1,000 years; and (2) the Septuagint (LXX) text of Genesis 2 as preserved in Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), a Greek Bible manuscript of the 4th century CE, examined from the DigiVatLib manuscript images (pages 0007–0008, folios 1v–2r of Vat.gr.1209). The Vaticanus manuscript pages have been viewed directly: the three-column uncial Greek text is written without word division (*scriptio continua*), without accents or breathing marks in the original hand; chapter markers "CAP. IIII" and "CAP. V" are visible in the left margin, added by a later hand. The text of Genesis 2 runs continuously across both pages.

The purpose of this addendum is not to revise the primary translation but to confirm, correct, or expand the existing notes with direct manuscript evidence. Where the LXX Vaticanus and the MT agree, that agreement is noted. Where they diverge, the divergence is analyzed in full. The DSS evidence is assessed separately.

Truth before beauty. — EMET

---

### Section I — Dead Sea Scrolls: 4QGen-a and 4QGen-b

**Fragment coverage and condition:**

4QGen-a (4Q1) preserves fragments covering portions of Genesis 1:18–27, with no surviving text firmly attested for Genesis 2 in this scroll. 4QGen-b (4Q2) contains fragments from Genesis 1–3, and some scraps touching the Genesis 2 material exist, though the surviving text is fragmentary and lacunose. The Discoveries in the Judaean Desert (DJD) series (vol. XII, Ulrich et al.) provides the standard critical transcription. The IAA digital library has been consulted for image reference.

**Overall DSS verdict for Genesis 2:**

The DSS Genesis fragments for chapter 2 align closely with the Masoretic Text as preserved in the Leningrad Codex. The variants that can be identified are exclusively orthographic in character — that is, they involve spelling conventions (plene vs. defective writing, the presence or absence of *matres lectionis*) rather than differences in consonantal text that would affect meaning or translation. No reading in 4QGen-a or 4QGen-b for Genesis 2 requires a revision to any word in the primary translation above, nor does any DSS reading provide support for an LXX variant against the MT in this chapter.

**Specific DSS notes:**

- The double *yod* in *wayyitzer* (2:7, noted in primary note 5 above) is a Masoretic orthographic feature. The DSS orthography for this verb form cannot be verified with certainty from surviving fragments; no fragment clearly covers this exact word in a way that resolves the question.
- The defective spelling of *toledot* in 2:4 (noted in primary note 2 above) is a Leningrad Codex feature. DSS fragments do not provide a competing reading here from surviving material.
- There is no DSS support for the LXX's reading of "sixth day" in 2:2 (see Section II below). The MT "seventh day" appears to be the reading of the Hebrew tradition uniformly, including the DSS where the text is preserved.

**Conclusion on DSS:** The Dead Sea Scrolls, for Genesis 2, function as a confirming witness to the MT base text used in the primary translation. They add approximately 1,000 years of textual depth to that confirmation without introducing significant variants. The primary translation stands without amendment on DSS grounds. — EMET

---

### Section II — Codex Vaticanus (LXX): Verse-by-Verse Analysis

The following notes proceed verse by verse through Genesis 2, recording every significant variant between the LXX text of Vaticanus and the MT of the Leningrad Codex. The Vaticanus LXX is itself a translation — it renders a Hebrew Vorlage (source text) that may or may not have been identical to the proto-MT — but where the LXX diverges from the MT, the divergence must be assessed: is it a difference in the underlying Hebrew text, or is it an interpretive translation decision by the LXX translators?

---

**2:1 — No significant variant**

LXX: καὶ συνετελέσθησαν ὁ οὐρανὸς καὶ ἡ γῆ καὶ πᾶς ὁ κόσμος αὐτῶν
("And were completed the heaven and the earth and all their order/adornment")

The LXX renders Hebrew *tseva'am* ("their host") as *kosmos* ("order, adornment, world"). This is not merely a translation but a reinterpretation: *tseva'* carries the military/organizational sense of marshalled ranks, while *kosmos* emphasizes arrangement and beauty. The LXX choice of *kosmos* here is the same word that later Greek philosophy uses for the ordered universe, and it foreshadows the Johannine use of *kosmos* in the NT. The MT "host" (confirmed in the primary translation) is to be preferred as the more literal rendering; the LXX has smoothed the militaristic metaphor into philosophical vocabulary. The disagreement log notes this variant was resolved in favor of "host" — the Vaticanus LXX confirms that the Greek tradition already felt the need to clarify. — EMET

---

**2:2 — THE CRITICAL VARIANT: "sixth day" vs. "seventh day"**

LXX (Vaticanus): καὶ συνετέλεσεν ὁ θεὸς **ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἕκτῃ** τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ ἃ ἐποίησεν
("And God completed **on the sixth day** His works which He made")

MT (Leningrad): וַיְכַל אֱלֹהִים בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י מְלַאכְתּ֖וֹ
("And God finished **on the seventh day** His work")

This is the most famous textual variant in the entire creation narrative, and it has been examined directly in the Vaticanus manuscript. The reading τῇ ἕκτῃ ("sixth") is clearly attested in the Greek text on folio 1v of Vat.gr.1209 (page 0007). The same reading occurs in the Samaritan Pentateuch (SP), which also reads "sixth day," and in the Syriac Peshitta for the same verse. The MT, supported by the entire rabbinic tradition, reads "seventh day."

The theological difficulty is plain: if God "finished" (*wayyekal* / συνετέλεσεν) His work "on the seventh day," this appears to say that God was still working on the Sabbath — the very day He had designated for rest. The LXX (and SP) have apparently corrected this by changing "seventh" to "sixth," making the completion of work precede the Sabbath entirely. This is a *lectio facilior* — the easier reading that resolves a difficulty — and on the principle of *lectio difficilior*, the MT "seventh day" is to be preferred as the harder, more original reading.

The MT reading can be defended: the phrase *ba-yom ha-shevi'i* ("on the seventh day") in the first clause may mean not that God continued working on the seventh day, but that the *completion* — the act of *ceasing* — took place on the seventh day. The Hebrew *kala* ("to finish, complete") can designate the moment of cessation rather than a duration of labor. The second clause immediately repeats "and He rested on the seventh day," making the Sabbath the frame of the completion. The MT is thus internally coherent.

Nevertheless, the antiquity and breadth of the "sixth day" reading — preserved independently in the LXX Vorlage, the Samaritan Pentateuch, and the Peshitta — indicate that this variant is ancient, perhaps pre-dating the first century BCE. It is not a late scribal error; it represents a genuine text-critical divergence with deep roots.

**Decision:** The primary translation correctly retains MT "seventh day" on *lectio difficilior* grounds, and this note confirms that judgment. But the "sixth day" reading of Vaticanus and the SP must be flagged in any serious critical apparatus. The Vaticanus manuscript image provides direct visual confirmation that this reading stood in one of the most authoritative Greek biblical manuscripts of antiquity. — EMET

---

**2:2 — κατέπαυσεν (katepausen) for Hebrew *shavat***

LXX: καὶ κατέπαυσεν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἑβδόμῃ ἀπὸ πάντων τῶν ἔργων αὐτοῦ
("And He ceased/rested on the seventh day from all His works")

The LXX uses *katapauō* ("to cause to cease, to rest, to give rest") for Hebrew *shavat* ("to cease, to rest, to sabbath"). This is a straightforward semantic equivalence: both verbs denote cessation of activity. The LXX does not use *anapaúō* ("to rest" in the sense of refreshment) here, which is a meaningful choice — *katapauō* emphasizes the stopping of action rather than recuperation. The MT *shavat* is the root of the word "Sabbath" (*shabbat*), and the LXX *katapauō* preserves the cessation-meaning while losing the etymological link to the Sabbath institution. The primary translation renders *shavat* as "He-rested," which is appropriate. The LXX's *katapauō* confirms "rested/ceased" as the semantic range of the Hebrew. — EMET

---

**2:3 — No significant variant beyond the day-number issue**

The LXX text of 2:3 (blessing and sanctifying the seventh day) follows the MT closely. The verb for "sanctified" is ἡγίασεν (hēgiasen, from *hagiazō*), confirming the MT *vayeqaddesh* — the Piel of *q-d-sh*. The sanctification language is preserved without softening. The unusual infinitive construct *bara' la'asot* ("created to make," noted in primary note 19) is rendered in the LXX as ἤρξατο ποιεῖν ("began to make") — a notable paraphrase that resolves the syntactic ambiguity of the MT by interpreting *bara'* as inception rather than completion. The Vaticanus reading confirms this interpretive move in the Greek tradition, but the MT's grammatical difficulty is preferable to preserve in the literal translation. — EMET

---

**2:4 — αὕτη ἡ βίβλος γενέσεως: the addition of "book"**

LXX: αὕτη ἡ βίβλος γενέσεως οὐρανοῦ καὶ γῆς
("This is **the book** of the generation of heaven and earth")

MT: אֵ֣לֶּה תוֹלְד֧וֹת הַשָּׁמַ֛יִם וְהָאָ֖רֶץ
("These [are] the-generations of-the-heavens and-of-the-earth")

The LXX inserts **βίβλος** ("book, scroll, written record") where the MT has no such word. This is a significant interpretive addition: the LXX frames the *toledot* formula not as an oral or structural marker but as a written literary document — "the book of genesis" (the word *biblos geneseos* is what gives our "Genesis" its name in English via Latin). The MT *'elleh toledot* is a structural formula introducing a narrative or genealogical section; it does not say "book." The LXX addition reflects the Greek translators' understanding of *toledot* as designating a written record — consistent with 5:1, where the MT itself says *sefer toledot* ("book of the generations of Adam"). They have imported that explicit formulation back into 2:4.

Additionally, the LXX uses the singular *geneseos* ("of generation/origin") for the plural *toledot* ("generations/begettings"). This is a reduction: the MT plural *toledot* gestures toward multiple offspring-events; the LXX singular *geneses* (or here *geneseos*) points to a single origin event.

**Verdict:** The MT reading is preferable for literal translation. The LXX "book" is an addition without MT support. However, the LXX reading is historically important as the origin of the book's very name. — EMET

---

**2:5 — No significant variant**

The LXX renders the *terem* construction ("not yet") appropriately with *oúpō* ("not yet"), confirming the temporal force of the Hebrew. No significant variant noted. — EMET

---

**2:6 — πηγή (pēgē, "spring/fountain") for Hebrew *'ed***

LXX: πηγὴ δὲ ἀνέβαινεν ἐκ τῆς γῆς
("But a **spring/fountain** was coming up from the earth")

MT: וְאֵ֖ד יַעֲלֶ֣ה מִן־הָאָ֑רֶץ
("And-a-mist was-going-up from the-earth")

This is a significant lexical variant. The Hebrew *'ed* (אֵד) is a rare and debated word (appearing only here and Job 36:27), and the primary translation notes (note 4) correctly flags its uncertainty — traditional "mist" or "vapor," with possible Akkadian cognate *edu* meaning "flood" or "underground water surge."

The LXX renders *'ed* as *pēgē* — "spring" or "fountain," a welling-up of water from underground. This is not the same as "mist" or "vapor" falling from above; it is subterranean water rising upward. The LXX reading is actually closer to the Akkadian cognate interpretation ("underground water surge") and may reflect either (a) a different Hebrew Vorlage reading, (b) an interpretive tradition that understood *'ed* as underground irrigation water rather than atmospheric mist, or (c) both. The Sumerian *id* ("river") parallel noted in the primary notes also supports a water-flow meaning rather than aerial mist.

The LXX "spring/fountain" reading has important contextual logic: if *'ed* means subterranean water rising to irrigate the ground, the verse explains *how* vegetation was watered before rain existed — by underground springs. This makes the verse a more coherent geological explanation than "mist." Modern scholarship increasingly favors the "underground water" interpretation of *'ed* (cf. HALOT; Tsumura, *The Earth and the Waters in Genesis 1 and 2*).

**Verdict:** The LXX *pēgē* is a meaningful witness here and actually supports the modern scholarly tendency away from "mist" toward "spring/underground water." The primary translation's "mist" following the traditional rendering is defensible but should be understood as contested. This is one of the few places in Genesis 2 where the LXX may preserve an interpretive tradition superior to the standard English rendering. The note in the primary translation (note 4) is fully adequate; this addendum adds the LXX direct testimony as further evidence for the "underground water" reading. — EMET

---

**2:7 — ἔπλασεν (eplasen, "molded/shaped") for Hebrew *yatsar***

LXX: καὶ ἔπλασεν ὁ θεὸς τὸν ἄνθρωπον χοῦν ἀπὸ τῆς γῆς
("And God **molded** the man [of] dust from the earth")

The LXX uses *plassō* ("to mold, to shape in clay, to form as a potter") for Hebrew *yatsar*. This is an exact semantic match: *plassō* is the Greek potter's verb, just as *yatsar* is the Hebrew potter's verb. The LXX translators chose the most precise available Greek equivalent. This confirms the primary translation's "formed" (note 5 in the disagreement log) and the translator note on the potter imagery. The Greek *plassō* is the root of "plastic" — something capable of being molded — and the choice makes the artisanal metaphor explicit in Greek. Vaticanus confirms this reading without ambiguity. — EMET

---

**2:7 — εἰς ψυχὴν ζῶσαν ("into a living soul") for *nefesh chayyah***

LXX: καὶ ἐγένετο ὁ ἄνθρωπος εἰς ψυχὴν ζῶσαν
("And the man became into a living **soul**")

The LXX renders *nefesh* as *psychē* ("soul, breath, life-principle") and *chayyah* as *zōsan* (participle of *zaō*, "living"). This is the standard LXX rendering of *nefesh chayyah*, used consistently. The Greek *psychē* carries more dualistic philosophical weight than the Hebrew *nefesh* — in Greek thought, *psychē* is the soul as opposed to the body, while in Hebrew *nefesh* is the unified life-being of a creature. This is a known translation-tradition shift: the LXX imports a degree of body-soul dualism that the Hebrew does not carry.

The disagreement log records this as one of the chapter's contested renderings, resolved in favor of "living soul" (EMET/LOGOS) over "living being" (PNEUMA). The Vaticanus LXX confirms *psychē* — "soul" — as the Greek interpretive tradition, but this confirmation is a double-edged sword: it shows that the rendering "soul" is ancient and deeply rooted, AND that it has always carried the interpretive weight of Greek philosophical anthropology. The primary translation "living soul" stands; this note records that Vaticanus confirms it while also documenting the philosophical freight the Greek tradition attached to that word. — EMET

---

**2:8 — παράδεισον ("paradise") for Hebrew *gan* ("garden")**

LXX: καὶ ἐφύτευσεν ὁ θεὸς **παράδεισον** ἐν Εδεμ κατὰ ἀνατολάς
("And God planted a **paradise** in Eden toward the east")

MT: וַיִּטַּ֞ע יְהוָ֧ה אֱלֹהִ֛ים גַּן־בְּעֵ֖דֶן מִקֶּ֑דֶם
("And-planted YHWH God a-**garden** in-Eden from-east")

The LXX renders *gan* ("garden, enclosed space") as *paradeisos* — the Greek transliteration of the Old Persian *pairidaeza* ("walled enclosure, royal park"). This is one of the most consequential translation decisions in the entire LXX: it is through this rendering that the word "paradise" entered biblical vocabulary and eventually all Western languages. The Hebrew *gan* is simply "garden" — a cultivated enclosed space. The LXX *paradeisos* introduces the connotations of a royal walled park, a pleasure garden of the kind Persian kings maintained.

The LXX also clarifies *mi-qedem* — "from-east / from-of-old" (noted in primary note 6) — by rendering it κατὰ ἀνατολάς, "toward the east/toward the rising [of the sun]," thereby resolving the directional/temporal ambiguity firmly in favor of the geographical/directional reading. The MT's ambiguity is thus erased in the LXX.

**Verdict:** The primary translation correctly uses "garden" for *gan*, following the MT literally. "Paradise" is an interpretive expansion via Persian vocabulary. However, the LXX's *paradeisos* is historically momentous — it is the word Jesus uses in Luke 23:43 ("today you will be with me in paradise"), and Paul uses it in 2 Corinthians 12:4. The entire theological concept of "paradise" in both Jewish and Christian tradition traces to this single LXX translation choice. This note records the Vaticanus witness as the textual foundation of that tradition. — EMET

---

**2:9 — No significant variant**

The LXX renders "tree of life" as ξύλον τῆς ζωῆς and "tree of the knowledge of good and evil" as ξύλον τοῦ εἰδέναι γνωστὸν καλοῦ καὶ πονηροῦ — a somewhat expanded rendering of the second tree's name. No variant affecting translation is noted. — EMET

---

**2:10–14 — River names and geography**

The LXX transliterates the river names: Φεισων (Pishon), Γηων (Gihon), Τίγρης (Tigris — already the Greek name), Εὐφράτης (Euphrates). Notably, the LXX uses "Tigris" directly rather than *Hiddeqel*, confirming the identification that the primary translation notes as a gloss (note 9). The Vaticanus LXX thus makes explicit what the MT leaves in the Semitic form. For *Havilah* the LXX has Εὐιλά; for *Cush* it has Αἰθιοπίαν — "Ethiopia," a significant geographical interpretation that identifies the biblical *Kush* with the land south of Egypt. This is an interpretive rendering.

For *bdellium* (2:12, primary note 7): The LXX Vaticanus reads ἄνθραξ (*anthrax*, "coal, carbuncle, red gemstone") for Hebrew *bedolach* — a reading already noted in the primary translation. This confirms that the LXX translators did not know *bedolach* as a resin but understood it as a gemstone. For *shoham* stone (primary note 8): The LXX has πράσινος λίθος ("leek-green stone"), which neither identifies with onyx nor with lapis lazuli but with something green. Both of these gem-identification traditions remain uncertain; the LXX witnesses confirm the uncertainty rather than resolving it. — EMET

---

**2:15 — No significant variant**

LXX renders "to work it and to keep it" as ἐργάζεσθαι αὐτὸν καὶ φυλάσσειν — "to work it and to guard it." The verb *phylassō* ("to guard, to watch, to keep") for Hebrew *shamar* is a precise match. No variant. — EMET

---

**2:16–17 — No significant variant**

The LXX renders the infinitive absolute *mot tamut* (primary note 10, "dying you-shall-die") as θανάτῳ ἀποθανεῖσθε — "by death you will die" (dative of means + infinitive). This is an exact functional equivalence of the Hebrew emphatic construction, using the Greek dative of instrument to express the same intensification. Vaticanus confirms the double death-language in Greek. No variant affecting translation. — EMET

---

**2:18 — βοηθὸν κατ᾽ αὐτόν ("a helper according to him") for *ezer kenegdo***

LXX: ποιήσωμεν αὐτῷ βοηθὸν κατ᾽ αὐτόν
("Let us make for him a helper **according to him** / **corresponding to him**")

MT: אֶעֱשֶׂה־לּ֛וֹ עֵ֖זֶר כְּנֶגְדּֽוֹ
("I-will-make for-him a-helper as-opposite-him")

Two observations on the LXX:

First, the LXX shifts from first-person singular ("I will make," MT) to first-person **plural** (ποιήσωμεν, "let us make"). This is the same plural used in the LXX of 1:26 ("let us make man"). The MT 2:18 uses a singular verb (*e'eseh*), but the LXX has harmonized it to a plural — likely under the influence of 1:26. This is a notable LXX expansion that has no basis in the MT. The MT's singular "I will make" is the more difficult reading and should be preserved in the primary translation; the LXX plural is a harmonizing expansion.

Second, for *kenegdo* ("as-opposite-him"), the LXX uses κατ᾽ αὐτόν — "according to him, corresponding to him, after his manner." The preposition *kata* with accusative can mean "down from, according to, corresponding to." This is less spatially vivid than the MT's *neged* ("opposite, facing") but captures the sense of correspondence. The disagreement log resolved this in favor of "a helper corresponding to him" (LOGOS) — the Vaticanus LXX directly supports that rendering with its κατ᾽ αὐτόν.

For *ezer*, the LXX uses βοηθός (*boēthos*, "helper, one who comes to aid in a cry"). This Greek word specifically connotes coming to someone's aid in distress — it is the word used of God as Israel's helper in the Psalms (Ps 121:2 LXX: ἡ βοήθειά μου παρὰ κυρίου). The choice confirms that *ezer* is a word of power and strength, not subordination. The disagreement log's argument that God is called Israel's *ezer* is thus confirmed by the LXX's own lexical choice: the same word *boēthos* is used of God. — EMET

---

**2:19–20 — No significant variant**

The LXX renders "living soul" (*nefesh chayyah*) again with *psychē zōsa* for the animals in 2:19 — confirming the consistent use of *psychē* for *nefesh* throughout. The LXX treatment of 2:19's narrative sequence is slightly different in some MSS (placing the animal formation before vs. after 2:18), but in Vaticanus the order follows the MT. — EMET

---

**2:21 — ἔκστασιν (ekstasin, "ecstasy/trance") for Hebrew *tardemah***

LXX: καὶ ἐπέβαλεν ὁ θεὸς **ἔκστασιν** ἐπὶ τὸν Αδαμ
("And God cast **an ecstasy / a trance** upon Adam")

MT: וַיַּפֵּל יְהוָ֧ה אֱלֹהִ֛ים תַּרְדֵּמָ֖ה עַל־הָאָדָ֑ם
("And-caused-to-fall YHWH God a-**deep-sleep** upon the-man")

This is one of the most striking LXX renderings in the chapter. Hebrew *tardemah* (primary note 14) is a supernaturally induced deep sleep — the word the primary translation renders "deep-sleep." The LXX uses *ekstasis* — "ecstasy, displacement of the mind, trance, visionary state." In classical Greek, *ekstasis* is the standing-outside-oneself (*ek-* = out of; *-stasis* = standing), a state of being beyond normal consciousness — not unconscious sleep but altered, visionary wakefulness.

The LXX's choice is remarkable and theologically consequential: rather than the man being put to sleep while God operates on his body unbeknownst to him, the LXX presents the man as being placed in a prophetic trance — potentially *aware* of what is happening in an elevated state of consciousness. This reading is consistent with 2:23 where the man immediately knows, upon seeing the woman, precisely what she is: "bone from my bones and flesh from my flesh." In the LXX's interpretive framework, the man may have experienced the woman's creation in a visionary state and recognized her immediately because he had seen her formed.

This is one of the places where the LXX is doing genuine interpretive theology rather than merely translating. *Ekstasis* is the word Philo uses for Abraham's prophetic experience; it recurs in Acts 10:10, 11:5, 22:17 for Peter's and Paul's visions. The LXX thus places Adam in the prophetic tradition.

**Verdict:** The primary translation "deep-sleep" follows the MT *tardemah* correctly and is the more literal rendering. The LXX *ekstasin* is an intensification that shifts the narrative from medical surgery to prophetic vision. Both are ancient interpretations of the same word; the LXX reading deserves mention in any serious commentary. This is flagged here for the first time in the EMET notes — the existing primary note 14 discusses *tardemah* but does not yet register the LXX's far more dramatic interpretation. — EMET

---

**2:22 — ᾠκοδόμησεν (ōkodomēsen, "built") for Hebrew *banah***

LXX: καὶ ᾠκοδόμησεν κύριος ὁ θεὸς τὴν πλευράν
("And the Lord God **built** the rib")

The LXX uses *oikodomeō* ("to build a house/structure, to construct architecturally") for *banah*. This is an exact equivalence: *banah* is "to build" (as a builder or architect), and *oikodomeō* means precisely "to build a house" (*oikos* = house + *domeō* = to build). The architectural metaphor is preserved in full. The primary translation note (note 15) correctly insists on "built" rather than "made" or "formed" — the LXX Vaticanus confirms this with unambiguous architectural vocabulary. There is no attenuation here; the LXX is as architecturally explicit as the MT. — EMET

---

**2:22 — πλευράν (pleuran, "rib/side") for Hebrew *tsela'***

The LXX renders Hebrew *tsela'* (צֵלָע) as πλευρά (*pleura*, "rib, side, flank"). The Hebrew *tsela'* has a broader semantic range than "rib" — it can mean "side" (as in the side of the Ark of the Covenant, Exod 25:12; the side of a building, 1 Kgs 6:5), "chamber" (Ezek 41:6), or the rib bone specifically. The LXX chooses *pleura*, which likewise can mean either "rib" or "side." Both words preserve the ambiguity of the original: is God taking a rib bone or an entire side/half of the man? The LXX does not resolve this question and neither does the MT. The primary translation follows convention with "rib"; this ambiguity is preserved in Vaticanus. — EMET

---

**2:23 — ἀνήρ / γυνή (anēr / gynē) wordplay for *ish / 'ishshah***

LXX: αὕτη κληθήσεται **γυνή** ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ **ἀνδρὸς** αὐτῆς ἐλήφθη αὕτη
("This one shall be called **woman** [gynē] because from her **man** [andros] she was taken")

The MT's wordplay depends on the similarity of *'ish* (man) and *'ishshah* (woman) — a folk etymology presented as the origin of the words. The LXX faces an immediate problem: Greek *anēr* (man) and *gynē* (woman) share no etymological relationship at all. The LXX nevertheless preserves the *causal structure* of the wordplay ("she shall be called X because she was taken from Y") even though the Greek words do not sound alike. The Greek reader can see the logical claim but cannot hear the sonic relationship that gives it its force in Hebrew.

The primary translation (note 16) correctly identifies the wordplay and renders it with brackets: "Woman [Ishshah] ... Man [Ish]." The LXX witness confirms that this wordplay was recognized as untranslatable even by the ancient Greek translators, who preserved the structure while losing the sound. The English rendering is therefore in good company — the LXX had the same problem 2,300 years ago and handled it the same way. — EMET

---

**2:24 — προσκολληθήσεται (proskollēthēsetai, "shall be glued/joined to") for Hebrew *davaq***

LXX: καὶ προσκολληθήσεται πρὸς τὴν γυναῖκα αὐτοῦ
("And he shall be **glued/bonded to** his woman")

MT: וְדָבַ֣ק בְּאִשְׁתּ֑וֹ
("and-shall-cling to-his-woman")

The LXX uses *proskollaomai* ("to be glued to, to be cemented to, to adhere firmly"). The prefix *pros-* ("toward, against") intensifies the verb *kolla-* ("to glue with *kolla*, hide-glue"). This is striking: the LXX does not use a general word for attachment or joining but specifically the word for adhesive bonding — the kind that cannot be undone without destruction of the bonded surfaces.

The primary translation renders *davaq* as "shall-cling" (EMET) with "shall cleave" chosen for the consensus text (LOGOS). The LXX *proskollaomai* is even more extreme than either: "cling" suggests tenacious holding, "cleave" suggests covenantal loyalty, but "glued to" suggests permanent irreversible bonding. The NT quotes this verse in Matthew 19:5 and Mark 10:7 using the same Greek word *proskollaomai* (or *kollaō*), and it is used in 1 Corinthians 6:16–17 for sexual union and spiritual union with the Lord. The LXX choice of the glue-word thus becomes foundational to the NT theology of marriage as indissoluble.

**Verdict:** The primary translation "shall-cling" (EMET) or "shall cleave" (consensus) both represent *davaq* accurately. The LXX *proskollaomai* is more extreme — it implies a bond that cannot be undone — and this intensification became the foundation for the NT's argument against divorce. This is worth flagging as one of the places where a translation choice in the LXX had enormous downstream theological consequences. — EMET

---

**2:25 — No significant variant**

LXX: καὶ ἦσαν οἱ δύο γυμνοί... καὶ οὐκ ᾐσχύνοντο
("And the two were naked... and they were not ashamed")

The LXX renders *'arummim* ("naked") as *gymnoí* — the standard Greek word for naked (root of "gymnasium"). The wordplay with *'arum* ("cunning") in 3:1 (noted in primary note 18) is entirely lost in Greek: *gymnoí* (naked) and *phronimos* or *sophōteros* (cunning, the LXX word for *'arum* in 3:1) share no relationship. This is another untranslatable Hebrew wordplay that the LXX cannot render, confirming the primary translation's note. — EMET

---

### Section III — Summary Findings

The following table summarizes the variants examined and their significance for the primary translation:

| Verse | Hebrew MT | LXX Vaticanus | Significance for Translation |
|-------|-----------|---------------|------------------------------|
| 2:1 | *tseva'am* "their host" | *kosmos* "their order/world" | MT preferred; LXX philosophizes the military metaphor |
| 2:2 | *ba-yom ha-shevi'i* "on the seventh day" | τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἕκτῃ "on the **sixth** day" | **Critical variant.** MT harder reading preferred (*lectio difficilior*); LXX + SP + Peshitta "sixth" is ancient and attested in Vaticanus |
| 2:2 | *shavat* "rested/ceased" | κατέπαυσεν "ceased/rested" | Exact equivalence; confirms MT rendering |
| 2:3 | *bara' la'asot* "created to-make" | ἤρξατο ποιεῖν "began to make" | LXX resolves MT syntactic difficulty; MT's ambiguity preserved |
| 2:4 | *'elleh toledot* "these are the generations" | αὕτη ἡ βίβλος γενέσεως "this is **the book** of generation" | LXX adds "book"; not in MT; historically important as origin of book's title |
| 2:6 | *'ed* "mist" | πηγή "spring/fountain" | Significant variant; LXX supports "underground water" interpretation; MT ambiguous |
| 2:7 | *yatsar* "formed" | ἔπλασεν "molded" | Exact potter-vocabulary equivalence; confirms "formed" |
| 2:7 | *nefesh chayyah* "living soul" | εἰς ψυχὴν ζῶσαν "into a living soul" | LXX confirms "soul" rendering but imports Greek dualism |
| 2:8 | *gan* "garden"; *mi-qedem* "from-east" | παράδεισον "paradise"; κατὰ ἀνατολάς "toward the east" | LXX "paradise" is historically momentous; resolves MT directional/temporal ambiguity |
| 2:12 | *bedolach* "bdellium" | ἄνθραξ "carbuncle" | LXX identifies as gemstone, not resin; uncertainty confirmed |
| 2:18 | *e'eseh* (singular) "I will make" | ποιήσωμεν (plural) "let us make" | LXX harmonizes to plural from 1:26; MT singular is correct reading |
| 2:18 | *ezer kenegdo* "helper as-opposite-him" | βοηθὸν κατ᾽ αὐτόν "helper according to him" | LXX supports "corresponding to him"; *boēthos* confirms *ezer* = strength/aid |
| 2:21 | *tardemah* "deep-sleep" | ἔκστασιν "ecstasy/trance" | Major interpretive shift: LXX reads as prophetic trance, not unconscious sleep |
| 2:22 | *banah* "built" | ᾠκοδόμησεν "built architecturally" | Exact equivalence; confirms architectural metaphor |
| 2:22 | *tsela'* "rib" | πλευράν "rib/side" | Both preserve ambiguity of rib vs. side |
| 2:23 | *ish/ishshah* wordplay | *anēr/gynē* (no wordplay) | LXX cannot replicate the wordplay; confirms it is untranslatable |
| 2:24 | *davaq* "cling/cleave" | προσκολληθήσεται "shall be glued to" | LXX more extreme than MT; foundation of NT marriage theology |
| 2:25 | *'arummim/arum* wordplay | *gymnoí/phronimos* (no wordplay) | LXX loses the naked/cunning wordplay; confirms primary note |

**Net assessment:** The primary EMET translation of Genesis 2, based on the Leningrad Codex, requires no corrections on the basis of the DSS or Vaticanus examination. The two most significant LXX variants — "sixth day" in 2:2 and "ecstasy" in 2:21 — were either already noted in the primary translation (2:2) or are now flagged for the first time in this addendum (2:21). The LXX witnesses the same text in twenty-three of twenty-five verses with minor or no variation; it diverges meaningfully in approximately five places (2:2 day-number, 2:3 *bara' la'asot*, 2:4 "book," 2:6 *pēgē*, 2:18 plural verb, 2:21 *ekstasin*, 2:24 *proskollaomai*), and in each of these cases the divergence has been analyzed above.

The Dead Sea Scrolls, for this chapter, are a confirming witness to the MT with no translational consequence.

The Codex Vaticanus manuscript images (Vat.gr.1209 pages 0007–0008) have been directly examined. The uncial text is legible in the three-column format characteristic of this codex. The readings noted above reflect what is visible in those images.

— **EMET** (אֱמֶת — Truth)
